methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $2.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $250M - $400.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Palantir
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $400B+

private.name: Liquid AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2.35B

private.name: Twelve Labs
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $250M

data.narrative:
Field AI, founded by Ali Agha and Shayegan Omidshafiei, is a Series A stage AI & Software company currently valued at $2.0B post-money and having raised $405M to date. The valuation assessment draws from a peer set of 9 comparable companies—1 public (Palantir) and 8 private entities including Liquid AI, Twelve Labs, OpenAI, and xAI—all operating in the AI & Software sector at similar developmental stages.

Two valuation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach, grounded in the $2.0B last post-money valuation, applies a standard 2.0x step-up multiplier for Series A advancement, implying a valuation of $4.0B. The peer-median method across three same-sector, same-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $2.4B, though the peer set exhibits substantial dispersion, ranging from $250M (Twelve Labs) to $400.0B (Palantir). 

The triangulated valuation range spans $2.0B (low) to $4.6B (high), with a median of $3.2B. This range reflects moderate divergence between stage-progression and peer metrics. Key uncertainties center on the wide dispersion within the peer set—Palantir's $400B valuation materially skews comparability—and the relative youth of the sector, which limits the stability of stage-transition multiples. Field AI's current $2.0B valuation sits at the lower bound of this range, suggesting modest upside under normalized stage progression but significant dependency on actual revenue trajectory and market conditions.
